data.body:
PASS 1 review (T1 — prior-art / novel-architecture justification) for Cluster 1 (canonical-JSON/content-hash consolidation onto `_store/canonical.py`): CLEAN, no findings.

T1 bright-line check: the only candidate that could fire here is "novel architectural pattern" / "migration". Neither fires as a concern —

AFFIRMATIONS (T1 criteria passed):
- (a) Relevant prior art IS considered, not reinvented/repackaged. This consolidates ONTO the already-live `_store/canonical.py` seam (verified canonical.py:39-47 defines `canonical_str`/`canonical_bytes` with `ensure_ascii=False, sort_keys=True, separators=(",",":")`, with 6 production importers), rather than introducing a new abstraction. The plan explicitly forbids a new module and caps `canonical.py` LOC ("no new module"; "canonical.py stays well under the 800-LOC cap"). The established "extract shared helper / move duplicates onto the existing home" pattern is exactly the right prior art and is the one chosen.
- (b) No novel pattern is introduced, so anti-repackaging / Rule-of-Three is satisfied trivially-and-then-some: 5 near-identical encoders (the seam + signing.py, schema.py, conflict_resolver.py, mutation.py) are unified — well over the Rule-of-Three threshold. The module docstring even records the governing cross-implementation prior-art lesson verbatim (canonical.py:18-19: "the TUF/sigstore lesson: canonicalization across implementations is a footgun; standardize on one"), and deliberately keeps stdlib `json` rather than reaching for a third-party canonical-JSON lib (matches the module's dependency-free design note; the epic also explicitly rejects a `tenacity`-style external dep for the sibling cluster). No NIH, no repackaging.
- (c) The one capability/behavior assertion that matters ("preserves every site's exact output bytes") is RESOLVED, not merely asserted: each retained variant maps to a verified codebase fact (signing.py:198 already uses `ensure_ascii=False` → seam default; mutation.py:106 uses `ensure_ascii=True` → `ascii_only=True`; conflict_resolver.py:217-219 omits `ensure_ascii` (stdlib default True) and uses `default=str`), and AC #5 pins a frozen-snapshot characterization test (incl. non-ASCII / nested / unicode-key) plus a sign→verify round-trip.

No bright-line concern remains for T1 on this cluster. (Note: the load-bearing conflict_resolver `ascii_only=True` byte-divergence is squarely an A1/G6/G1G2 mechanism-correctness matter already surfaced repeatedly on this epic; it is not a T1 prior-art/novelty concern, so I do not re-raise it here.)
